sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “Restore Us, O God; Make Your Face Shine on Us”

heb.paragraph: "Restore us (hashîbēnū), O God; make your face shine on us, that we may be saved" (v.3). The refrain appears three times (vv.3, 7, 19), intensifying: "Restore us, O God" → "Restore us, God Almighty" → "Restore us, LORD God Almighty." Each repetition adds a divine title. The Shepherd of Israel (v.1), who leads Joseph like a flock, is asked to awaken and come to rescue. "You have made us an object of derision; our enemies mock us" (v.6).

notes.note: "Hear us, Shepherd of Israel, you who lead Joseph like a flock." The shepherd-king imagery from Psalms 23, 78, 79. God leads Israel as shepherd leads sheep.
"Restore us, O God; make your face shine on us, that we may be saved." The refrain: restoration = God’s shining face. Salvation is not political rescue but divine presence.
"How long will you be angry? You have fed them with the bread of tears. You have made us an object of derision." Tears as food; mockery as identity. The nation is defined by its suffering.

refs.note: Jesus as the good shepherd who lays down his life fulfills the psalm's shepherd invocation.
God himself searching for and tending his sheep develops the shepherd theology this psalm invokes.
Returning to the Shepherd and Overseer of souls echoes the psalm's plea for the shepherd's attention.

hist.context: Psalm 80 is a northern kingdom lament mentioning Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh (v.2) — Joseph tribes. This suggests composition after Assyria's conquest of the north (722 BC) or during its decline. The refrain 'restore us, O God; make your face shine' (vv.3, 7, 19) structures the psalm into three sections with escalating divine titles.

heb.paragraph: "You transplanted a vine (gefen) from Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it" (v.8). Israel as vine: God brought it from Egypt, cleared the ground, planted it (v.9), it took root and filled the land (v.9). But now: "Why have you broken down its walls?" (v.12). Boars ravage it, insects devour it (v.13). "Return to us, God Almighty! Watch over this vine, the root your right hand has planted, the son you have raised up for yourself" (vv.14–15). The "son" (בן, bēn) is both Israel and the messianic king.

notes.note: "You transplanted a vine from Egypt. It took root and filled the land. Its shade covered the mountains; its branches reached the Mediterranean." The vine metaphor: Israel’s growth from Egyptian slavery to Promised Land abundance. God is the gardener.
"Why have you broken down its walls? Boars from the forest ravage it." The garden is now unprotected. The walls are God’s decision to remove.

"Let your hand rest on the man at your right hand, the son of man you have raised up for yourself. Restore us, LORD God Almighty." The "son of man at your right hand" is messianic. The final refrain adds all three divine titles.

refs.note: Isaiah's vineyard song uses identical imagery of a vine planted that yields wild grapes.
Jesus as the true vine and believers as branches transforms the psalm's vine metaphor.
The parable of the tenants draws on the same vineyard-as-Israel tradition.

hist.context: The vine metaphor (vv.8-16) traces Israel's story: transplanted from Egypt, it spread from sea to sea, its branches reaching to the Euphrates. Now the walls are broken, boars ravage it, and fire burns it. The prayer for 'the son of man you have raised up' (v.17) was later read messianically but originally referred to Israel or its king.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God leads Israel as shepherd leads sheep…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYou have made us an object of derision to our neighbors, and our enemies mock us.ESVYou make us an object of contention for our neighbors, and our enemies laugh among themselves.NIVHow long, Lord God Almighty, will your anger smolder against the prayers of your people?ESVO LORD God of hosts, how long will you be angry with your people\u2019s prayers?

rec.text: ""Hear us, Shepherd of Israel, you who lead Joseph like a flock." The shepherd-king imagery from Psalms 23, 78, 79. God leads Israel as shepherd leads sheep."

rows.label: The Vine — 80:8–19
rows.text: Transplanted from Egypt. Walls broken. "Return to this vine." "The son at your right hand."

hebtext.note: "Restore us (hashîbēnū), O God; make your face shine on us, that we may be saved" (v.3). The refrain appears three times (vv.3, 7, 19), intensifying: "Restore us, O God" → "Restore us, God Almighty" → "Restore us, LORD God Almighty." Each repetition adds a divine title. The Shepherd of Israel (v.1), who leads Joseph like a flock, is asked to awaken and come to rescue. "You have made us an object of derision; our enemies mock us" (v.6).

"You transplanted a vine (gefen) from Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it" (v.8). Israel as vine: God brought it from Egypt, cleared the ground, planted it (v.9), it took root and filled the land (v.9). But now: "Why have you broken down its walls?" (v.12). Boars ravage it, insects devour it (v.13). "Return to us, God Almighty! Watch over this vine, the root your right hand has planted, the son you have raised up for yourself" (vv.14–15). The "son" (בן, bēn) is both Israel and the messianic king.

themes.note: Covenant peaks: Israel as God’s vine transplanted from Egypt. Prophecy: the "son of man at your right hand" is messianic. Mercy: the escalating refrain of restoration.
